questions.text: Where did Gautama Buddha deliver his first sermon?
questions.explanation: The first sermon (Dhamma Chakka Pavattana Sutta — 'Setting in Motion the Wheel of Dhamma') was delivered at Sarnath (near Varanasi) at the Deer Park (Mrigadava). This event is called Dhammachakkapavattana or the First Turning of the Wheel.

questions.text: The Fourth Buddhist Council was held during the reign of which king?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was held in Kashmir (~1st–2nd century CE) under the patronage of Kushana king Kanishka. It led to the formal split into Mahayana (greater vehicle) and Hinayana (lesser vehicle / Theravada) schools. The Third Council was under Ashoka at Pataliputra.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the Four Noble Truths of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The Four Noble Truths are: Dukkha (suffering exists), Samudaya (suffering has a cause — craving), Nirodha (suffering can be ended), Magga (the path — the Eightfold Path). Extreme asceticism is NOT the path; the Buddha rejected both extreme luxury AND extreme asceticism, choosing the Middle Way.

questions.text: Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism, was born in:
questions.explanation: Mahavira (Vardhamana) was born at Kundagrama near Vaishali (Bihar) in 599 BCE (traditional). He was a Kshatriya prince. He attained enlightenment (Kevala-jnana) at Jrimbhikagrama and died (Nirvana) at Pavapuri.

questions.text: Which of the following was NOT a republic (Gana-sangha) among the 16 Mahajanapadas?
questions.explanation: Magadha was a monarchy (rajatantra), not a republic. Among the 16 Mahajanapadas, Vajji (with Vaishali as capital) was the most powerful republic (gana-sangha). Malla, Kamboja, Shakya (Buddha's clan), and Koliya were also republics.

questions.text: The concept of 'Anekantavada' (many-sidedness of truth) belongs to:
questions.explanation: Anekantavada is the Jain philosophical principle that reality is complex and can be understood from many viewpoints — no single perspective captures the complete truth. It is also called Syadvada (doctrine of 'maybe'). This is distinct from Buddhist concepts like Sunyata (emptiness).

questions.text: The Tripitaka — the canonical Buddhist scripture — is written in which language?
questions.explanation: The Tripitaka (Pali Canon) is written in Pali — a vernacular language related to Sanskrit, probably similar to what was spoken in the Gangetic plain in Buddha's time. It comprises Vinaya Pitaka (monastic rules), Sutta Pitaka (discourses), and Abhidhamma Pitaka (philosophy).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly pairs a Buddhist Council with its location?
questions.explanation: Third Buddhist Council was held at Pataliputra (Patna) under Emperor Ashoka's patronage (~250 BCE). First Council = Rajagriha (Ajatashatru); Second Council = Vaishali; Fourth Council = Kashmir (Kanishka). Do not confuse these locations — it is a very common SSC trap.

questions.text: Which Mahajanapada had its capital at Rajagriha (Rajgir)?
questions.explanation: Magadha's capital was Rajagriha (Rajgir, Bihar) — later shifted to Pataliputra (Patna) under the Shishunaga and Nanda dynasties. Magadha became the most powerful Mahajanapada due to iron ore resources, fertile Ganga plains, and strong rulers like Bimbisara and Ajatashatru.

questions.text: The Digambara and Shvetambara sects of Jainism differ primarily on which issue?
options: Whether Mahavira was married or celibate
Whether monks should wear clothes or remain naked
questions.explanation: Digambara (sky-clad) Jains believe monks should be naked and that women cannot attain moksha in female birth; Shvetambara (white-clad) believe monks can wear white clothes and that women CAN attain moksha. The 19th Tirthankara Malli was a woman according to Shvetambaras — Digambaras dispute this.

questions.text: The 'Ajivika' sect was founded by whom and what was its key belief?
options: Makkhali Gosala; strict determinism (niyati) — no free will
questions.explanation: The Ajivika sect was founded by Makkhali Gosala (a contemporary and onetime associate of Mahavira). Its central belief was absolute determinism (Niyativada) — everything is pre-determined; free will is an illusion; liberation comes only after a fixed number of rebirths. The sect is now extinct.

questions.text: What are the 'Three Jewels' (Triratna) of Buddhism?
options: Right View, Right Speech, Right Action
questions.explanation: The Three Jewels (Triratna) or Three Refuges of Buddhism are: Buddha (the enlightened teacher), Dhamma (the teaching/truth), and Sangha (the community of monks and followers). Taking refuge in these three is the formal declaration of becoming a Buddhist.

Gautama Buddha was born at Lumbini (now in Nepal) around 563 BCE. The Four Great Events of Buddha's life: Birth = Lumbini; Enlightenment = Bodh Gaya; First Sermon = Sarnath; Death (Mahaparinirvana) = Kushinara (Kushinagar, UP).

questions.text: The Eightfold Path of Buddhism does NOT include:
questions.explanation: The Eightfold Path (Ashtangika Marga) consists of: Right View, Right Intention, Right Speech, Right Action, Right Livelihood, Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, Right Concentration. There is NO 'Right Caste' — Buddhism explicitly rejected the caste system.

questions.text: Vardhamana Mahavira attained enlightenment (Kevala Jnana) at which age?
questions.explanation: Mahavira attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience/enlightenment) at age 42, after 12 years of intense asceticism. He died (attained Moksha/Nirvana) at Pavapuri at age 72. This is frequently confused with Buddha's enlightenment at age 35.

questions.text: Which of the following is the Jain equivalent of the Buddhist 'Dhamma Chakka Pavattana' (First Sermon)?
options: Mahavira's first sermon at Pava after enlightenment
Mahavira renouncing his kingdom at age 30
questions.explanation: After attaining Kevala Jnana, Mahavira delivered his first discourse (Deshana) at Pava (Pavapuri area, Bihar) to a group that became his first disciples (ganadharas). This is the Jain equivalent of the Buddha's First Sermon at Sarnath.
